text.title: Quarterly Retainer Review Script Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the quarterly-retainer-review-script methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Call is 45 minutes. Hard stop. Segments: data 10 min, outcomes 15 min, blockers 10 min, renewal 10 min.
rule.rationale: Gainsight 2024 data: QBRs over 60 minutes correlate with lower renewal rates (-12 pp); 45 min forces preparation density.

rule.statement: Call ends with one of four explicit decisions named aloud by the client: continue / scale_up / scope_down / terminate. 'Let me think about it' is not a decision — push for a date by which they will name one.

rationale.source: Chris Voss, Never Split the Difference

rule.rationale: Open-ended endings produce churn invisible until renewal date passes.

rule.statement: First 10 minutes is data-only: hours used / hours bought, SLA % met, scope items shipped vs agreed. No interpretation. Numbers visible on screen.

rationale.source: Kahneman, Thinking Fast and Slow — anchoring

rule.rationale: Numbers shown first anchor in objective territory; narrative-first lets the client anchor on last-bad-week emotion.

rule.statement: If decision is scale_up or scope_down, the exact scope delta is written into decision-record JSON live during the call.

rationale.source: Mike Monteiro, Design Is a Job

rule.rationale: Verbal scope changes are the #1 source of next-quarter conflict in service businesses.

rule.statement: 1-page email follow-up arrives within 24h of the call: decision, scope delta, next-quarter dates, action items per side.
rule.rationale: HubSpot 2024 data: client-signed amendments arrive 3x faster with <24h follow-up.

list.item: One-shot project work — use a project-closure script instead
Pre-launch / first quarter of a new retainer — use kickoff review
Client delinquent on payments — collections call comes first
